recognitionTakeaway: 月 links the idea 'moon; month' from an ancient graphic origin through regular script into modern Chinese, Japanese Kanji, and Korean Hanja usage.

visuals.note: Regular-script prototype card exists. Historical Oracle/Bronze/Seal assets are attached when source-backed Wikimedia Commons SVGs are available; final publication still needs licensing review and specialist redraw approval.

taiwanExamples.translation: Basic Taiwan sentence using moon; month.

hongKongExamples.translation: Basic Hong Kong sentence using moon; month.

examples.translation: Basic Japanese sentence using moon; month.
Basic Korean sentence using moon; month.

history.originAnchor: 月 is taught here from its original idea: a picture of the moon, later written as 月 and extended to month.

stages.label: Oracle Bone Script
stages.historicalSound: Old Chinese sound not shown yet; add only after dedicated reconstruction review.

stages.label: Bronze Script
stages.changeNoteFromPrevious: Bronze inscriptions continue the ancient graph tradition after the oracle-bone period; final 月-specific redraw still required.
stages.historicalSound: Not shown in this seed record.

stages.label: Seal Script
stages.changeNoteFromPrevious: Seal script formalizes earlier graph traditions into a more standardized archaic written style.
stages.historicalSound: Not shown in this seed record.

stages.label: Clerical Script
stages.changeNoteFromPrevious: Clerical script flattens and regularizes earlier forms before later regular-script writing.
stages.historicalSound: Not shown in this seed record.

stages.label: Regular Script
stages.changeNoteFromPrevious: The regular form stabilizes as 月, still recognizable in modern focus-track usage.

structure.summary: 月 is handled as a basic graph/radical for 'moon; month'. It can appear as a standalone character and as a component in related characters.
structure.caveat: Seed record uses public reference sources; publication requires specialist paleography review and final source-backed glyph redraws.

usage.coreMeaningFirst: Start with 'moon; month', then compare how 月 is read and used across Mandarin, Traditional Chinese usage communities, Japanese, and Korean.
notes: Japanese often has separate Sino-Japanese on readings and native kun readings.
Korean examples separate the Hanja reading from the native Korean everyday word when they differ.
Modern examples are seed content and still need native-speaker review before publication.

sources.label: Chinese character classification reference
sources.citation: Chinese character classification explains the pictographic origin of many early Chinese characters and includes examples such as sun, moon, mountain, water, and wood/tree.

sources.label: Oracle bone script reference
sources.citation: Oracle bone script is the oldest attested substantial Chinese writing corpus and an ancestor of later Chinese-family scripts.

sources.label: Seal script reference
sources.citation: Seal script developed from earlier bronze/large-seal traditions and was standardized in Qin usage.

sources.label: Clerical script reference
sources.citation: Clerical script matured and became dominant during the Han dynasty, flattening and regularizing earlier forms.

sources.label: EVOBC dataset paper
sources.citation: Guan et al., 'An open dataset for the evolution of oracle bone characters: EVOBC', documents ancient-character evolution categories including oracle bone, bronze, seal, and clerical stages.

sources.label: Internal app-native prototype redraw
sources.citation: Internal schematic redraw used only for prototype testing; specialist review and source-backed final redraws are still required.

sources.label: Radical 74 / 月 reference
sources.citation: Radical 74 lists 月 as the moon/month radical with Mandarin yuè, Japanese getsu/gatsu and tsuki, and Korean 월 wol / 달 dal.

notes: Source-backed seed pass generated from public radical/script references.
Historical stage explanations are sourced, but final stage glyphs still require licensed/source-backed redraws.
Modern examples need native-speaker/editorial review before publication.
